jurisdictions.name: Australia
jurisdictions.key_finding: Broadest cross-sector pricing enforcement, entirely through general-purpose provisions that cannot reach undisclosed personalized pricing. The UTP Bill 2026 advances with language broad enough to reach surveillance pricing, aimed entirely at dark patterns and drip pricing.
jurisdictions.opening:
*Jurisdiction molecule for Paper 1.*

Australia's regulatory profile is defined by the distance between enforcement intensity and instrument design. The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission and the Australian Securities and Investments Commission have pursued pricing practices across retail, travel, and insurance with a combined penalty and remediation record exceeding AUD 815 million since 2018. Every enforcement action targets misleading price presentation (drip pricing, illusory discounts, loyalty discount misrepresentations) through general-purpose misleading conduct provisions (Australian Consumer Law section 18, ASIC Act section 12DA) not designed for algorithmic personalized pricing. In parallel, the ACCC's five-year Digital Platform Services Inquiry (2020 to 2025, final report June 2025) produced the analytical foundation for the Competition and Consumer Amendment (Unfair Trading Practices) Bill 2026, introduced in the House of Representatives on 1 April 2026. The Bill proposes a general prohibition on conduct that manipulates consumers or unreasonably distorts the environment in which consumers make decisions, with drip pricing and subscription trap provisions. Its language is broad enough to reach personalized pricing through the "unreasonably distorts the environment" formulation, but the legislative materials, consultation responses, and ACCC enforcement priorities do not identify personalized pricing as a target. The operative instruments remain general-purpose, the proposed instruments aim at conspicuous practices, and the Privacy Act's automated decision-making transparency obligation (effective December 2026) produces the weakest ADM provision in the dataset.

instruments.enforcement_mechanism: ACCC; up to AUD 50M or 3x benefit or 30% adjusted turnover per contravention

jurisdictions.name: California
jurisdictions.key_finding: The legislature failed to prohibit surveillance pricing twice; the attorney general pivoted to privacy law to reach what the competition statute explicitly excludes.
jurisdictions.opening: California's instrument set is organized around competition, targeting algorithmic coordination through antitrust reform (AB 325, Cartwright Act amendment) and enforcement (RealPage/Greystar nine-state coalition settlement). The legislature attempted general prohibition twice in 2025 and was blocked both times; a narrower retail-only version (AB 2564) advanced through committee in March 2026. The attorney general pivoted from antitrust to privacy law through a California Consumer Privacy Act investigative sweep, reaching for the proprietary personalized pricing that the enacted competition statute explicitly excludes. The resulting instrument set spans antitrust, privacy, and consumer protection, assembled from different legal authorities at different moments rather than designed as a coordinated response.

jurisdictions.name: Canada
jurisdictions.key_finding: Largest drip pricing enforcement in the dataset. The Competition Bureau explicitly identified its own inability to reach personalized pricing. Provinces legislate what federal law cannot.
jurisdictions.opening: Canada's regulatory response to algorithmic pricing is competition-dominated and federally constrained. The Competition Act, substantially amended in 2022 and 2024, provides the strongest enforcement infrastructure in the instrument set, with drip pricing penalties now judicially confirmed at CAD 38.9 million (Cineplex, Federal Court of Appeal, January 2026). The Competition Bureau's June 2025 discussion paper on algorithmic pricing is one of the few regulatory documents in the dataset that explicitly distinguishes dynamic pricing from personalized pricing and identifies the gap between coordination (reachable under the Act) and unilateral personalization (unreachable). Federal privacy law (PIPEDA) applies general consent and purpose limitation principles but lacks automated decision-making provisions, and Bill C-27, which would have introduced both privacy modernization and AI regulation, died twice on the Order Paper (January 2025 prorogation and the April 2025 election). The resulting federal vacuum has two provincial responses that formally address the core species but cannot operate nationally. Quebec's Law 25 (operative since September 2023) imposes automated decision-making transparency and human review obligations with no enforcement on pricing. Manitoba's Bill 49 (introduced March 2026) would prohibit personalized algorithmic pricing in retail, the first Canadian instrument specifically designed for the core species. The design logic is accumulated with a competition center of gravity and a federal-provincial dimension absent elsewhere in the dataset.

jurisdictions.name: China
jurisdictions.key_finding: Seven-year escalation from disclosure to prohibition. The only jurisdiction where each instrument tightened the same constraint. The first operative prohibition that reaches surveillance pricing.
jurisdictions.opening: China built seven instruments across five regulatory strategies in a deliberate escalation from disclosure to prohibition over seven years (2019 to 2026). The trajectory moves in a single direction, with each successive instrument tightening the constraint on the same practice rather than opening a new front. The Rules for Price Behavior on Internet Platforms, jointly issued by the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC), State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR), and Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) and operative since April 10, 2026, make China the first jurisdiction in the dataset with an operative prohibition designed to reach unilateral personalized pricing based on behavioral data. Rights-based instruments (Personal Information Protection Law Article 24) formally trust individuals, but enforcement across all instruments runs through the state.

instruments.name: Rules for Price Behavior on Internet Platforms (Dec 2025, eff. Apr 10, 2026)
instruments.enforcement_mechanism: NDRC, SAMR, CAC jointly; penalties per Price Law framework; mandatory self-inspection program

Interim period; major platforms conduct mandatory self-inspections
Rules take effect

jurisdictions.name: European Union
jurisdictions.key_finding: Eight instruments from different eras, no hierarchy among them. Approaching four years of Omnibus non-enforcement on personalized pricing.
jurisdictions.opening: The EU occupies every strategy in the topology except prohibition and incentive-based regulation, spanning disclosure, rights and liabilities, competition, and code-as-regulation, yet no single instrument in the set was designed for surveillance pricing. Eight instruments accumulated across different legislative eras and policy objectives (consumer protection, data protection, competition, platform governance, AI safety), producing a dense regulatory surface with no hierarchy and low internal coherence. The two instruments that formally reach the core species (Consumer Rights Directive Article 6(1)(ea) for disclosure and General Data Protection Regulation Article 22 for automated decision-making rights) have produced zero enforcement on algorithmic pricing in any member state, approaching four years after the Omnibus Directive's disclosure obligation took effect. The only enforcement momentum in the set flows through competition investigations under Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union Articles 101 and 102, which structurally require coordination or dominance and cannot reach unilateral personalized pricing by non-dominant firms.

instruments.name: Consumer Rights Directive Art. 6(1)(ea) (Omnibus Directive 2019/2161, eff. May 2022)
instruments.enforcement_mechanism: National consumer protection authorities; penalties up to 4% of turnover or EUR 2M; Consumer Protection Cooperation Network; no private right of action at EU level

timeline.event: Omnibus Directive 2019/2161 adopted
Member state transposition deadline
Provisions enter into force across member states
Zero enforcement actions on personalized pricing under Art. 6(1)(ea) in any member state (47 months, approaching four years)

instruments.name: GDPR Article 22 (eff. May 2018)
instruments.enforcement_mechanism: National data protection authorities; fines up to EUR 20M or 4% of global turnover; individual right to lodge complaint with DPA

timeline.event: GDPR adopted
GDPR takes effect, including Article 22

Court of Justice of the European Union rules in *SCHUFA Holding (Scoring)* (Case C-634/21), holding that credit scoring by an agency constitutes automated individual decision-making when a third party "draws strongly" on the score, expanding Article 22's scope to preparatory algorithmic outputs relied upon by downstream actors

CJEU rules in *Dun & Bradstreet Austria* (Case C-203/22), holding that data protection authorities or courts must weigh competing interests when controllers claim algorithmic outputs are trade secrets, establishing a framework for balancing transparency rights against trade secret protection
Zero enforcement actions applying Article 22 to personalised consumer pricing in any member state (eight years)

instruments.name: TFEU Articles 101/102 + 2023 Horizontal Guidelines
instruments.enforcement_mechanism: European Commission (DG Competition); national competition authorities; fines up to 10% of global turnover; private damages actions under Damages Directive

timeline.event: Treaty of Rome establishes competition provisions (now Articles 101/102 TFEU)

No formal decisions, statements of objections, or settlement outcomes published

jurisdictions.name: Germany
jurisdictions.key_finding: The EU's first enforcement order against algorithmic pricing (Bundeskartellamt v. Amazon, EUR 59M). Consumer-facing disclosure has produced zero enforcement in four years.
jurisdictions.opening: Germany layers a distinctive national regulatory architecture on top of the EU baseline. Nine instruments span four of the seven topology positions, but the weight concentrates in competition. The Bundeskartellamt (Federal Cartel Office) produced the EU's first enforcement order and monetary disgorgement against algorithmic pricing mechanisms (EUR 59M against Amazon, February 2026), combining sustained research output with escalating enforcement in a pattern no other national competition authority in the dataset has matched. Consumer protection instruments arrived as Omnibus Directive transposition and have produced zero enforcement on personalized pricing in nearly four years. The design logic is accumulated but directional, with two successive amendments to the Gesetz gegen Wettbewerbsbeschränkungen (Act against Restraints of Competition, GWB) in 2021 and 2023 progressively expanding the competition toolkit while consumer protection and data protection instruments arrived as EU requirements without domestic initiative.

jurisdictions.name: India
jurisdictions.key_finding: Enforcement responds to what goes viral (iPhone-vs-Android fare disparities). The two instruments that could structurally reach surveillance pricing are both inoperative.
jurisdictions.opening: India has no instrument that directly addresses surveillance pricing or algorithmic personalized pricing. What it has instead is a constellation of general-purpose consumer protection, competition, and data protection instruments, none designed for the problem, several being stretched toward it. The most significant regulatory activity is enforcement-led rather than legislative. The Central Consumer Protection Authority's January 2025 notices to Uber and Ola for differential pricing based on operating systems remain in the investigative phase with no findings issued. The Competition Commission's October 2025 market study acknowledged algorithmic collusion risks and recommended self-audits rather than enforcement. The instrument set is fragmented across five regulators (CCPA, CCI, IRDAI, RBI, Data Protection Board) with limited coordination, though the IRDAI's April 2026 directive requiring insurance platforms to comply with CCPA dark patterns guidelines is the first cross-regulatory signal. The two proposed instruments that could most directly reach algorithmic pricing have both stalled. The Draft Digital Competition Bill 2024, modeled on the EU's Digital Markets Act, was placed in cold storage following inter-ministerial reassessment with no timeline for revival. The DPDP Rules 2025 algorithmic impact assessment obligation remains inoperative because no Significant Data Fiduciary has been designated. India's regulatory posture toward surveillance pricing is best described as reactive and enforcement-triggered, in the sense that regulators respond to consumer complaints and global trends rather than addressing the structural practice.

jurisdictions.name: Indonesia
jurisdictions.key_finding: The dataset's only direct-action instrument: ride-hailing tariff caps driven by driver protests, not regulatory theory. A per se prohibition on price discrimination has been dormant since 1999.
jurisdictions.opening:
*Jurisdiction molecule for Paper 1.*

Indonesia has no instrument that directly targets surveillance pricing or algorithmic personalized pricing. Its regulatory landscape is a patchwork of general-purpose laws (consumer protection, competition, data protection, e-commerce platform governance), none of which were designed for or have been applied to personalized pricing. What distinguishes Indonesia from Singapore (its ASEAN neighbor with a similarly absent targeted regime) is not the gap itself but the nature of the gap. Singapore's absence reflects a deliberate pro-innovation design choice backed by sophisticated voluntary governance infrastructure. Indonesia's absence reflects institutional fragmentation and legislative lag. Multiple agencies (the Business Competition Supervisory Commission or KPPU, the Ministry of Trade, the Financial Services Authority or OJK, and the Ministry of Communication and Digital or Komdigi) govern adjacent spaces with overlapping but uncoordinated mandates. The most distinctive feature of Indonesia's regulatory landscape is the ride-hailing tariff cap regime (Permenhub 12/2019), which represents a direct-action intervention on algorithmic pricing in transportation, not framed as surveillance pricing regulation but functionally constraining platforms' ability to deploy unchecked dynamic pricing. The regime is in active ferment, with a competition law amendment in parliamentary deliberation, implementing regulations for the PDP Law still pending, AI governance being formalized through presidential regulations, and KPPU's first major digital-market pricing enforcement (the Indonesian Fintech Lenders Association or AFPI interest rate cartel, March 2026) signaling increased institutional capacity. Indonesia is a jurisdiction in transition from absence toward framework, yet as of April 2026, the framework has not arrived.

jurisdictions.name: Netherlands
jurisdictions.key_finding: The dataset's most unified pricing authority deploys exclusively soft instruments. Two market investigations, guidelines, supervisory dialogue. Zero enforcement.
jurisdictions.opening: The Netherlands layers a distinctive national regulatory architecture on top of the EU baseline. Where the EU provides the general framework (GDPR, CRD, UCPD, AI Act, DMA), the Netherlands adds three things the EU alone does not. First, a combined competition-and-consumer-protection authority (ACM) that has launched the first EU national-level market investigation specifically into computer-controlled consumer pricing (aviation sector, 2025) and a second into grocery pricing (supermarkets, September 2025), representing the most investigation-intensive approach to consumer pricing in the dataset. Second, a financial sector regulator (AFM) that has produced the most developed supervisory framework for personalized pricing in insurance anywhere in the EU, including a 2021 exploratory study and a 2025 deep dive finding that almost half of non-life insurers charge loyal customers higher margins. Third, an institutional coordination infrastructure (the SDT platform and the AP's Department for the Coordination of Algorithmic Oversight) that connects the multiple regulators whose mandates touch algorithmic pricing. The design logic is accumulated but institutionally coordinated, and the instruments come from different eras and legal bases. The Netherlands has invested more than any other EU member state in making its regulators talk to each other about algorithms while choosing to investigate rather than enforce.

jurisdictions.name: New York
jurisdictions.key_finding: Deployed disclosure and prohibition simultaneously for different sectors, not as sequential escalation. Both now face First Amendment challenge.
jurisdictions.opening: New York enacted disclosure for general commerce and prohibition for rental housing in the same legislative session (November-December 2025), occupying both ends of the regulatory topology simultaneously rather than escalating from one to the other. Both operative instruments now face First Amendment challenges from different industry actors. A March 2026 legislative package would extend prohibition to general commerce and grocery stores, signaling further movement.

jurisdictions.name: Singapore
jurisdictions.key_finding: Unified voluntary governance of unmatched coherence across four regulators. Same design logic as China, opposite direction. Thirteen instruments, zero enforcement.
jurisdictions.opening:
*Jurisdiction molecule for Paper 1.*

Singapore has no instrument that directly targets surveillance pricing or algorithmic personalized pricing. Its regulatory landscape is composed entirely of general-purpose instruments (data protection, competition law, consumer protection), none of which were designed for or have been applied to personalized pricing. What distinguishes Singapore from other jurisdictions with similarly absent targeted regulation is not the gap itself but the deliberate governance architecture around AI that nonetheless leaves pricing unaddressed. The Model AI Governance Framework, the PDPC Advisory Guidelines on AI Systems, the MAS FEAT Principles, the AI Verify toolkit, and the Model AI Governance Framework for Agentic AI collectively constitute the most sophisticated voluntary governance infrastructure in the dataset, emphasizing transparency and fairness in AI decision-making while containing no binding obligation relevant to pricing. The absence of regulation is a design choice, not an oversight, and Singapore's "pro-innovation, principles-based" approach deliberately avoids sector-specific or practice-specific AI regulation, channeling governance through existing laws supplemented by non-binding frameworks.

jurisdictions.name: South Korea
jurisdictions.key_finding: Asia-Pacific's densest instrument set, largest fines. The courts are reversing the major enforcement precedents. No instrument has reached personalized pricing.
jurisdictions.opening: South Korea occupies a distinctive position in the dataset. It is the only Asia-Pacific jurisdiction to have enacted binding dark pattern regulation reaching pricing transparency (the E-Commerce Act amendments, operative February 2025), an automated decision-making right in data protection law (the Personal Information Protection Act Article 37-2, operative September 2024), and a comprehensive AI governance framework (the AI Basic Act, operative January 2026). The instrument set is the densest in the region, with nine instruments spanning four of the seven regulatory strategies. The Korea Fair Trade Commission (KFTC) has imposed the Asia-Pacific's largest fines against algorithmic manipulation, including KRW 162.8 billion against Coupang (August 2024) for search algorithm manipulation and KRW 27.1 billion against Kakao Mobility (February 2023) for dispatch algorithm self-preferencing. None of these instruments specifically targets surveillance pricing or algorithmic personalized pricing. Each responds to a different international trend rather than a domestic diagnosis of personalized pricing as a regulatory problem. The regulatory architecture is fragmented across the KFTC (competition and consumer protection), the Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC, data protection), the Financial Services Commission and Financial Supervisory Service (FSC/FSS, financial services), and the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT, AI governance), with no coordinated enforcement strategy on algorithmic pricing. Since October 2025, the courts have begun contesting the KFTC's algorithmic enforcement precedents, with the Supreme Court reversing the Naver Shopping self-preferencing decision and the Seoul High Court overturning the Kakao Mobility dispatch algorithm fine, leaving the enforcement record more fragile than the instrument density suggests.

jurisdictions.name: United Kingdom
jurisdictions.key_finding: Earliest sector-specific prohibition in the dataset (FCA price walking ban, 2022). Post-Brexit divergence from the EU is now accelerating.
jurisdictions.opening: The UK occupies a distinctive position on the topology as the only jurisdiction to have enacted a sector-specific prohibition on algorithmic price discrimination (the FCA price walking ban in home and motor insurance, operative since January 2022) while relying on disclosure-adjacent enforcement and competition powers for general commerce. The instrument set is institutionally concentrated, with the CMA and FCA together holding the enforcement architecture, reinforced by the DMCCA 2024 which gave the CMA direct consumer enforcement powers (effective April 2025) that it deployed at scale in its first year (14 investigations, GBP 4.7 million in fines, 157 advisory and warning letters by April 2026). Post-Brexit regulatory divergence from the EU is now operative rather than prospective, with the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025's data protection provisions taking effect on February 5, 2026, loosening the UK GDPR's automated decision-making restrictions while the EU's equivalent provisions remain intact. The CMA's first competition investigation targeting algorithmic pricing coordination (hotel chains sharing data through a common analytics platform, February 2026) confirms the enforcement pattern observed across the dataset, in which competition instruments reach coordination but not unilateral personalized pricing.

jurisdictions.name: United States (Federal)
jurisdictions.key_finding: $3.8 billion in quantified renter harm, 250 retailers identified, enforcement only against coordination. The 2025 change of administration severed the consumer protection track.
jurisdictions.opening: The US federal government has no statute, regulation, or binding rule that directly addresses surveillance pricing or personalized consumer pricing. Federal regulatory activity operates through antitrust enforcement (DOJ Sherman Act actions against algorithmic collusion), consumer protection authority (FTC Section 5 unfairness/deception, deployed through studies and enforcement rather than rulemaking), and proposed legislation (three bills targeting algorithmic pricing, none advancing past committee). The 2025 change of administration produced a sharp discontinuity, severing the consumer protection track while preserving the antitrust track. The DOJ's RealPage prosecution (initiated under Biden) continued to settlement under the Trump DOJ, while the FTC's surveillance pricing study was effectively suspended by the incoming Chair. In March 2026, the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform opened a Republican-led investigation into surveillance pricing in the travel and hospitality sectors, signaling bipartisan congressional interest even as legislation stalls. The result is a jurisdiction where the most consequential regulatory action is antitrust enforcement against coordination, the consumer protection dimension is stalled, and legislative proposals face a hostile congressional environment.

jurisdictions.name: US States (Composite)
jurisdictions.key_finding: The legislative wave doubled in volume between 2025 and 2026 while maintaining a 100% failure rate for general prohibition; grocery-sector prohibition passed in two states (New Mexico, Maryland), confirming that industry opposition determines which regulatory framings survive the political economy filter.
jurisdictions.opening: Between 2024 and April 2026, US state legislatures produced a rapid, accelerating legislative wave targeting surveillance pricing, growing from 10 bills in 2024 to 51 across 24 states in 2025 to over 40 in the first quarter of 2026 alone (already outpacing the full 2025 total). The wave has now produced two enacted prohibition instruments beyond New York: New Mexico's HB 285 (signed March 2026, the first grocery-sector prohibition) and Maryland's Protection from Predatory Pricing Act (passed April 2026, the second). General prohibition of surveillance pricing across all sectors maintains a 100% failure rate across both legislative cycles, with every such bill dying in committee or on the floor. The dominant proposed strategy remains general prohibition, yet the only strategies that have produced enacted instruments are disclosure (New York A6765, profiled separately) and sector-specific grocery prohibition (New Mexico, Maryland). In February 2026, the United Food and Commercial Workers International Union launched a national campaign providing model legislation (surveillance pricing prohibition combined with electronic shelf label ban) to at least twelve states, marking the wave's transition from decentralized spontaneity to coordinated campaign in the grocery sector. Industry opposition (led by the California Chamber of Commerce, TechNet, National Retail Federation, and Chamber of Progress) continues to determine which strategies survive. The Congressional attempt to preempt state action through a ten-year moratorium on state AI regulations in the budget reconciliation bill failed when the Senate stripped it 99-1, preserving the state legislative pathway.
